proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies this as a compilation of the Raiden Fighters arcade shooting games, emphasizing pixel graphics, high-tempo action, scoring systems, and online rankings. That strongly indicates a classic arcade shoot-'em-up/bullet-hell style collection. However, that gameplay identity does not map cleanly to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype in the provided list, and forcing it into an unrelated archetype would be inaccurate.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: classic arcade vertical Raiden shmup, short replayable runs, bullet-heavy survival, score modes, vertical screen support, and leaderboard-focused modern reissue features.

Another Raiden arcade vertical shooter with score attack, boss-focused modes, rankings, and collection-era extras; slightly less direct than Raiden IV only because it is a single enhanced entry rather than a trilogy-style package.
Excellent format match as a retro arcade shmup collection built around multiple vertical shooters, TATE support, short runs, high difficulty, and mastery-driven replay.

A major vertical bullet-hell benchmark with strong overlap in arcade stage runs, challenge, and scoring appeal, but it is a single Cave-style bullet-hell release rather than a retro compilation or Raiden-style package.
A focused retro shooter compilation with multiple classic arcade shmups, short high-intensity runs, and leaderboard-style replayability.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe Afterplace as a single-player open-world adventure focused on free exploration, hidden paths, secrets, dungeons, monster fighting, NPC conversations, and multiple endings driven by player choices. The strongest identity signals are open-world exploration, handcrafted discovery, quest/exploration progression, branching narrative outcomes, and active melee-oriented combat against monsters. RPG elements like weapon upgrades, armor purchases, and choice-driven endings support a secondary western_narrative_rpg read, but the dominant presentation in the evidence is open-world action-adventure rather than a deeper stat- or party-driven RPG. Perspective is not explicitly stated in the stored evidence, but the remaining evidence is strong enough to support the archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong fit on top-down exploration, hidden routes, minimal handholding, secrets, and light action-adventure structure. More puzzle/manual-decoding and tougher combat than Afterplace, but the discovery loop is highly aligned.

Shares top-down exploration, shortcuts, secrets, NPC interactions, varied weapons, and upgrade-driven progression. More systems-heavy, combat-technical, and pressure-driven due to its timer, but still a close gameplay neighbor.

Good match for retro-styled top-down roaming, branching paths, secrets, and action combat. It is notably harsher, faster, and lighter on dialogue/choice structure than Afterplace, making it a slightly less direct fit.

Overlaps on top-down melee combat, secrets, exploration, and light stat/ability progression. However, it is more structured, boss-driven, and punishment-focused than Afterplace’s freer open-world wandering.
Dreamlike top-down exploration with loose structure, hidden areas, odd characters, and discovery-first progression over hard combat.

removed_candidates: The Legend of Zelda: Link's Awakening

title: Path of Mystery: A Brush with Death

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently frames the game around a non-combat, chapter-based mystery investigation: revisiting a decades-old murder, gathering clues, interviewing suspects, and uncovering the truth. Metacritic explicitly labels it as a Visual Novel, while the description emphasizes authored story progression, detective focus, and no combat-driven loop. The evidence for hidden-object gameplay is weaker because clue-finding is mentioned, but explicit hidden-object scenes or object-list puzzle structure are not. Overall the strongest fit is visual_novel with detective-mystery framing.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: The Centennial Case: A Shijima Story
staged_neighbors.requested_title: The Centennial Case: A Shijima Story
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Chapter-based murder mystery adventure built around interviewing suspects, collecting clues, and solving cases across different time periods.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Famicom Detective Club: The Missing Heir + Famicom Detective Club: The Girl Who Stands Behind
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Famicom Detective Club: The Missing Heir
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Classic linear Japanese murder investigation adventure with manor-setting exploration, suspect questioning, and clue-driven progression.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: PARANORMASIGHT: The Seven Mysteries of Honjo
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Paranormasight: The Seven Mysteries of Honjo
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Supernatural detective visual novel centered on unraveling murders through dialogue, investigation, and authored mystery progression.
Story-heavy detective adventure mixing investigation, interviews, and puzzle solving in a tightly authored mystery campaign.
Ensemble-cast mystery adventure focused on investigating a closed-circle crime, examining evidence, and identifying the true culprit.

missing_must_include_titles: The Centennial Case: A Shijima Story
Famicom Detective Club: The Missing Heir
Paranormasight: The Seven Mysteries of Honjo

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and metadata evidence consistently describe a story-driven action RPG centered on Velvet and her companions, indicating a campaign-focused JRPG structure rather than a loot or open-world identity. The description explicitly emphasizes a revenge journey across Midgand, a cast of companions, authored story themes, destination markers, and a combat system built around choosing Artes, performing combos, and collecting Souls for attack barrages. Stored signals also strongly support party-based play: party_tactics, party_management, party_control, and party_management_loop all appear in the audit payload with relatively high confidence. Metacritic labels it Action RPG, while Steam categories add single-player and co-op support, which fits Tales-style auxiliary co-op without displacing the core single-player campaign identity. Some facets like exact topology and perspective are inferred from the remaster/store framing and franchise-typical presentation cues in the text rather than being directly spelled out, so confidence is solid but not maximal.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The original release shares the same core campaign, party system, real-time Artes combat, progression, and world structure.
Another modern Tales action JRPG with real-time party combat, Artes chaining, character swapping, and a story-led adventure structure.
Very close series neighbor with the same Tales formula of real-time battles, party management, overworld exploration, and story-driven progression.
Shares the recognizable Tales loop of party-based action combat, combo-heavy Artes use, skits, towns/dungeons, and long-form JRPG progression.
A party-oriented action RPG with fast combo combat, ability-driven chaining, character bonds, and a strongly narrative campaign.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Towerborne as a side-scrolling 'Action RPG Brawler' with classic combo-based combat, four weapon styles, fast responsive encounters, and up-to-4-player co-op. The loop is hub-to-expedition-to-return: venture out from the Belfry, fight through enemies, then return to turn in quests, reforge gear, and customize builds. That supports beat_em_up as the core gameplay identity, with secondary loot_action_rpg elements from gear, crafting resources, build flexibility, and progression. The current computed taxonomy appears noisy in places, but the underlying store text strongly supports a co-op side-scrolling brawler rather than platformer or traditional fighter.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: 2D co-op brawler/RPG hybrid built around classes, loot, repeated runs, and combo-heavy side-scrolling combat.
Four-player side-scrolling beat-'em-up with character progression, weapon variety, and a light RPG loop around stage runs.
Modern side-scrolling action RPG beat-'em-up with gear upgrades, hub returns, and cooperative progression through enemy-filled stages.
Co-op side-scrolling brawler that adds character growth, equipment, and expanded progression on top of classic beat-'em-up combat.

current_live_neighbors.title: BADLAND: Game of the Year Edition

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 3D hack-and-slash action RPG centered on fast-paced combat, a 20+ character roster, and 50+ story missions. The Steam detailed description gives the strongest identity signals: '3D HACK-AND-SLASH REBORN,' 'mix and match flashy combat techniques,' 'evolvable abilities,' and 'create an unstoppable team' all support a character-action core with party-management elements rather than a pure solo combo-action game. The presence of a primary story mode following Mask of Truth, a base system for interacting with companions and strengthening characters, and an online/single-player Dream Arena side mode supports a campaign-driven, mission-based structure with secondary JRPG-story-RPG traits. Evidence is strong enough to accept, but not strong enough to place it in a more specific action-RPG subtype from the allowed list.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor with the same core mission-based anime hack-and-slash structure, playable roster, and story adaptation format.
Anime action game centered on flashy crowd combat, multiple playable characters, story missions, and character-specific progression.
Party-based action RPG that mixes musou-style battles with story progression, team management, and character growth between missions.
Mission-structured action game with a large cast, relationship/base interactions, unit development, and tactical party management elements.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Pogui as a side-scrolling platformer with hazardous levels, running/jumping/dashing, stamina-managed movement, and world-by-world stage progression. Metacritic explicitly calls it a 'side-scrolling precision platformer' in retro pixel art style, which strongly supports precision_platformer as the core identity. The game appears level-based across 5 themed worlds with handcrafted challenges and traversal-focused play. Enemy presence and 'defeat this nightmare' language indicate light action elements, making action_platformer a reasonable secondary archetype, but the dominant loop is precise movement and obstacle navigation rather than combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Tight side-scrolling precision platforming built around dash-based movement, hazard-heavy stages, and short challenge-focused level design.
Fast, exacting 2D platforming with quick retries, compact obstacle courses, and a strong emphasis on movement mastery over exploration.
Level-based 2D platformer with themed worlds, fluid movement, enemy-filled stages, and frequent mechanic twists from one level set to the next.
Cute but demanding side-scrolling platformer with world-by-world progression, precise jumps, enemies, and a polished retro-inspired feel.
Another compact, difficult 2D precision platformer focused on dangerous stages, exact timing, and survival through hostile environments.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies Dice A Million as a 'roguelike deckbuilder' built around randomly generated runs, combining 120+ dice for synergies, equipping passive rings, choosing among multiple hands/classes, and overcoming escalating debts and bosses. That strongly matches a run-based deck-construction game with buildcraft, metaprogression/unlocks, tactical optimization, and boss encounters. Although the theme is dice rather than literal cards, the core gameplay identity is clearly deckbuilder/card-battler adjacent, and card_battler is the closest supported archetype in the taxonomy.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Explicit dice-deck-building roguelike with run-based drafting, dice-pool construction, tactical turns, and heavy synergy optimization; closest gameplay match in the set.
Turn-based deckbuilding roguelike centered on manipulating dice to power drafted spells across repeated encounters; very close loop despite a more combat-spell framing.

Run-based dice roguelike with class-specific builds, equipment synergies, and tactical combat. Slightly less direct because it is more equipment/combat-encounter driven than deckbuilder-score engine driven.
Strong overlap in luck-pushing, debt-clearing run structure, build synergies, and break-the-economy scoring, but the slot-machine symbol engine is more adjacent than a true dice/deck combat builder.
Roguelike deckbuilder where dice replace cards, with run-based drafting, synergies, and tactical turn-by-turn optimization.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify this as a Reigns-style swipe-driven narrative/choice game: 'swipe right, swipe left,' 'thousands of storyline combinations,' 'navigate the moral maze,' puzzle-solving, unlocking deeds/characters, and leveling up Dandelion's career. That is strong evidence for a branching choice-centric design, but the allowed archetype set does not include a clear Reigns/interactive-choice/narrative-card archetype. Available action RPG or beat 'em up candidates in the current taxonomy debug payload are contradicted by the actual text, which does not support third-person melee action as the core gameplay loop. Because the gameplay identity is clear yet does not map cleanly and safely to an allowed archetype, rejection is the most accurate outcome.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The original template: left/right card swipes, balancing consequences, short runs, and branching narrative outcomes.
A direct follow-up built on the same swipe-choice structure, stat management, unlocks, and event-chain storytelling.
Another licensed Reigns variant using the same core loop of binary decisions, multiple rulers, and run-based branching stories.
Extends the same Reigns formula with swipes, narrative event chains, character encounters, and light strategic resource balancing.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes only that this is a single-player action/adventure remake tied to the City Hunter license. The descriptions are almost entirely promotional and story-focused, with no concrete gameplay information about perspective, traversal, combat loop, mission structure, world topology, or progression. Because the gameplay identity is not described with enough specificity to distinguish among multiple action-adventure archetypes, the evidence is too thin for a reliable taxonomy mapping.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Anime-styled side-scrolling action with mission structure and character-driven single-player progression.
Modern revival of a classic action format with straightforward single-player stage-based brawling.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored description consistently identifies LOVE as a challenging retro platforming game built around 16 levels, speedrun mode, competitive scoring/leaderboards, and a unique checkpoint-respawn mechanic. That strongly supports precision_platformer over broader platformer archetypes: the core loop is stage clearing through difficult platforming challenges rather than combat, exploration, or collection. Evidence supports level-based progression, platform-navigation mechanics, score-attack replayability, mastery-heavy input demands, and no meaningful combat emphasis.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: High-difficulty level-based precision platforming with instant restarts, tight movement, and strong speedrun appeal.
Retro-styled minimalist platformer focused on harsh challenge, exact movement, and compact room-by-room obstacle design.
Pure mechanical platforming with minimal visuals, demanding stages, leaderboard competition, and an emphasis on mastery.
Precision platforming with dense challenge rooms, forgiving respawns, and a strong replay/speedrun loop.
Tough retro-inspired platformer centered on dense hazard courses, collectible-driven replay, and constant retry flow.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is materially contradictory. Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently present Supernova as a space-themed Suika-style matching puzzle focused on combining planets for high score, while the OpenCritic description instead describes a sci-fi MOBA/strategy game with upgradeable armies, commanders, and crafting from drops. These imply fundamentally different gameplay identities, and neither line of evidence is strong enough to cleanly map into a supported Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype with confidence. Because the bundle appears to conflate different titles or contains unreliable metadata, classification should be rejected.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct match for the presumed anchor identity: same drop-and-merge puzzle loop, same escalating object-combination structure, and similar score/space optimization.
Strong puzzle pedigree and shared falling-piece spatial pressure, but its line-clearing tetromino play is materially different from Suika-style physics merging.
Near-direct Suika-like comp built around dropping, bouncing, and merging fruit in a confined playfield for score.
Falling-piece score-attack puzzle that rewards chain planning and space control in a compact board.
Strong puzzle crossover comp for players who like score pressure, stacking decisions, and spatial optimization.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Royal Vermin as a 2D local platform fighter for 2-4 players, explicitly 'inspired by the best platform fighters.' The core loop is match-based PvP: players climb, throw, use ranged chargeable projectiles, knock opponents out of collapsing arenas, and aim to win rounds in fast, chaotic matches. Side-scrolling presentation, duel-focused combat, dominant combat presence, and win-match goals strongly support traditional_fighter over platformer; platforming is present but serves the fighting format rather than precision challenge progression. Party-game qualities are notable through local same-screen multiplayer and chaotic comeback-focused design, but they are secondary to the fighter identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest comp: multiplayer platform-fighter matches focused on knockbacks, ring-outs, stage hazards, and easy-to-learn party play.
Free-to-play platform fighting with accessible controls, side-view arenas, item-based weapon/projectile play, and chaotic multiplayer rounds.
A more mechanics-driven platform fighter sharing the same side-on arena combat, aerial movement, edge pressure, and knockout-based match flow.
Another accessible platform brawler centered on fast local-versus matches, stage control, launches, and party-friendly crossover chaos.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Ella Stars as an 'action-rhythm adventure' built around 30 original songs, with explicit rhythm gameplay, epic/intense boss battles, perfect combo finishers, adjustable difficulty, and single-player play. That strongly supports rhythm_game as the core identity, with timing-based play and boss-focused encounters as the main loop. Evidence for exact camera/topology is not explicit, so those fields are left empty rather than inferred.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong fit: action combat explicitly synced to music, authored stages, and stylish boss encounters align well with Ella Stars' action-rhythm and boss-focused loop, though Hi-Fi Rush is a larger 3D character-action game.

Music-driven action-adventure with colorful boss fights and combat built around musical energy is close in audience and structure, even if its combat is less strictly rhythm-timing gated than Ella Stars.

Strong song-by-song battle identity and memorable rhythm-centric encounters fit the boss-driven music angle well, but Everhood plays more like pattern-dodging musical battles than melee action-rhythm adventure.

Clear rhythm-game overlap with original tracks, hand-drawn presentation, single-player structure, and accessible difficulty options, but it lacks Ella Stars' combat-heavy and boss-centric action layer.
Rhythm-timed action platforming built around authored stages, original music, and repeated boss fights with a stylish, character-driven presentation.

title: Welcome to Doll Town

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a single-player, third-person horror experience centered on exploration, mystery-solving, eerie puzzles, and escape rather than combat. Key phrases include 'Third-Person Exploration,' 'solve eerie puzzles,' 'uncover the truth,' 'slow-burn descent into horror,' and reality/dream dissolution, which strongly align with psychological horror. The cursed village, haunted dollhouses, environmental storytelling, and emphasis on dread and mystery support a narrative-driven psychological horror classification more strongly than action_horror or survival_horror, both of which lack concrete combat evidence here.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very strong match on third-person town exploration, puzzle solving, narrative-driven progression, and psychological mystery, even if it has more direct combat than the anchor likely does.
Close fit for slow-paced third-person psychological horror centered on investigation, environmental storytelling, and puzzles, with limited combat pressure.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Fatal Frame: Maiden of the Black Water
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Fatal Frame: Maiden of Black Water
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strong supernatural Japanese horror comp with third-person exploration, cursed locations, mystery unraveling, and doll imagery, though its camera-based combat is more central.
Reasonable fit through third-person horror, tension, evasion, and story-gated exploration, but it is more pure survival-horror and stealth-threat driven than the anchor.
Third-person survival horror built around investigation, puzzles, and evasion rather than combat, with a compact, tense narrative structure.

missing_must_include_titles: Fatal Frame: Maiden of Black Water

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies SealChain as a single-player roguelike centered on item-linking, formations/seals, random events, character builds, talent trees, and combat against monsters. However, the available text does not establish a strong fit to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a card battler despite combinatorial item synergies, not clearly turn-based tactics despite strategy language, and not supportable as co_op_action_roguelite because the evidence indicates single-player only. The Metacritic 'Top-Down Shoot-'Em-Up' label is too weak and isolated to anchor a shooter archetype. Overall the gameplay identity is real but falls between the available archetypes, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strongest blend of action roguelike combat and inventory-layout-driven progression. The spatial equipment management is a close mechanical neighbor to SealChain's item-linking and formation optimization.

Excellent match on run-based item placement, adjacency synergies, and build-defining inventory optimization. It lands slightly lower because its core combat loop is more turn-based/deckbuilding-oriented than SealChain's apparent action emphasis.
A solid action roguelike benchmark for randomized runs, monster combat, and extreme item-combo build mutation. Less precise because it lacks the anchor's spatial item-formation puzzle layer.

Fits top-down run-based combat, distinct characters, heavy item/stat stacking, and fast power scaling. Weaker than above options because its wave-survival structure and simpler equipment logic are farther from SealChain's formation/linking identity.
Roguelite progression centered on deliberate placement and combinatorial synergies, capturing the anchor’s formation optimization more than standard action roguelites do.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Stillborn Slayer as a single-player dark fantasy action roguelike with fast hack-and-slash combat, procedural runs, brutal difficulty, boss fights, dodging/rolling, and a grim horror-inflected tone. However, the evidence does not firmly establish a supported taxonomy archetype from the allowed list. The strongest identity is run-based action roguelite, but the available archetype list only includes co_op_action_roguelite, which contradicts the explicitly single-player framing. Alternative fits such as soulslike_action_rpg or character_action remain too weak because perspective is not established, progression is described as run/repetition-based rather than RPG progression, and the copy also emphasizes bullet-hell roguelike structure over a stable campaign RPG format. Because the title's core gameplay identity appears to fall between unsupported/insufficiently evidenced archetypes, classification should be rejected for now.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on dark fantasy action roguelike structure, punishing melee combat, dodge timing, trap-filled runs, and repeated procedural boss-focused attempts.
Essential benchmark for fast top-down action roguelike runs built on repeated deaths, enemy pattern mastery, bosses, and build variation, though it is more narrative and meta-progression heavy.
Strong fit on action-roguelite runs, skilled dodge-centric combat, bosses, and bleak atmosphere, but its waking-life/social progression makes the structure less purely combat-run driven.
Reasonable match through hack-and-slash roguelite runs, procedural dungeons, and boss combat, but it is more story-driven, character-RPG oriented, and partly co-op framed than the anchor.
Dark fantasy action roguelite with rapid melee combat, dodge-based survival, boss fights, and repeated procedural runs.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 30+ hour JRPG with a party-based adventure, explorable isometric dungeons, a world map linking towns and quests, and explicit turn-based battles where the player chooses each party member's actions and plans turns tactically. The text also emphasizes non-linear progression after the prologue, several branching endings, relationship/trust systems with party members and towns, and equipment/resource loops for commissioning gear. That combination strongly supports jrpg_story_rpg as the core identity, with some tactical overlap from its turn-based party combat.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on party-based turn-based JRPG structure, continent exploration, dungeon travel, political conflict, and gear/crafting progression. Less distinctive on town trust/supply systems, but the core play identity is highly aligned.

Excellent fit on classic overworld-to-dungeon JRPG flow, turn-based party combat, rebellion/war themes, recruitable allies, and headquarters/town progression. Slightly broader and more ensemble-driven, but very compatible for audience expectation.

Strong story-driven JRPG comp with party management, turn-based battles, and anti-regime campaign momentum. It appears more linear and less exploration-and-systems driven than the anchor, so the fit is good but not top tier.

Shares traditional turn-based JRPG combat, party progression, town interaction, and open destination choice. However, its eight-protagonist structure and chapter-based progression are a looser match for the anchor’s party campaign and town-supply progression loop.
Isometric party RPG with kingdom restoration, town progression, character bonding, and tactical turn-based battles.

removed_candidates: Atelier Sophie 2: The Alchemist & The Mysterious Dream

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored publisher/store text consistently describes Ys X: Proud Nordics as the definitive edition of an action RPG adventure centered on fast-paced combat, exploration of a large island/map area, boss fights, and an authored story campaign. The copy emphasizes 'fast-paced combat,' 'Cross Action,' 'Mana Action,' new bosses, a high-difficulty dungeon, and broad map/exploration content, all of which support a single-player fantasy action-RPG identity. The current computed taxonomy pointing to traditional_fighter is contradicted by the actual descriptive text, which clearly frames a narrative-driven exploratory action RPG rather than a PvP or match-based fighter. The strongest fit in the available taxonomy is open_world_fantasy_action_rpg, though the world structure reads more semi-open than fully open world.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Proud Nordics is the enhanced definitive edition of the original, retaining the same core combat, exploration, progression, and story foundation.
Another Falcom action RPG centered on fast real-time combat, party-driven exploration, map completion, and frequent boss encounters.
Very close in pace and structure: traversal-focused action RPG combat, dungeon crawling, character abilities, and story-led progression.
Falcom action RPG with quick combo-based combat, character swapping, dungeon runs, and progression built around combat plus narrative segments.
A recognizable action RPG comp with flashy real-time party combat, special team attacks, boss fights, and linear-to-open area exploration.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly indicates a casual single-player merge/falling-block style puzzle game focused on dropping, stacking, and merging breads for score, with light customer-request interactions and leaderboard play. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a general merge puzzle, falling-block puzzle, or score-attack puzzle category. The computed candidate of hidden_object_puzzle is not supported by the described gameplay loop, which centers on spatial dropping/merging rather than hidden-object searching. Because the game's identity is clear but does not cleanly fit any allowed archetype, it should be rejected rather than force-mapped.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Nearly identical core loop: drop items into a container, merge matching pieces into larger ones, prevent overflow, and chase high scores in short replayable runs.
Strong on falling-piece spatial management, topping-out pressure, and replayable score-focused puzzle flow, but line-clearing Tetris rules are materially different from merge-evolution play.
Direct Suika-like: drop fruit into a container, use physics positioning, merge matching pieces, and chase higher scores before topping out.
Clean high-score merge puzzle benchmark centered on combining like pieces into higher values, but with swipe movement instead of falling physics.
Shares arcade puzzle pressure, stack management, and quick-session replayability, though its match/chaining rules are less like-for-like than Suika-style merging.
